name: AnГЎlisis comparativo de estrategias de trading en oro
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person is developing algorithmic trading strategies for XAUUSD (Gold) on the M5 timeframe, targeting prop firm funding accounts (specifically FundingPips with $5,000 initial capital). They brought two existing strategy versions вҖ” v1.1 and v2 вҖ” each with multiple parameter sets and both backtest (BK) and forward test (FR) results exported from MT5 Strategy Tester as Excel files. The core goal throughout was identifying which strategy version and parameter set offers the best probability of passing a prop firm challenge, then building an improved v3 EA incorporating lessons learned.

Claude performed a comprehensive quantitative analysis of all ten Excel files (four from v1.1, six from v2), extracting key metrics including profit factor, win rate, drawdown, recovery factor, Sharpe ratio, and per-year P&L breakdowns from the MT5 deals sections. The critical finding was that v1.1 SET 2 forward test is the strongest candidate, with an ISвҶ’OOS profit factor ratio of 1.18 вҖ” the only configuration where out-of-sample performance genuinely exceeded in-sample. By contrast, v2 showed systematic OOS degradation of 18% across all three sets, attributed to the SPS filter overfitting to the training period and an unrealistically large TP of 395 pips for M5 timeframe. A visual dashboard was generated comparing all configurations across profit factor, win rate, drawdown, and prop firm suitability scores.

Based on the analysis, Claude built ORB_BreakoutEA_v3.mq5 using v1.1 SET 2 as the structural foundation, incorporating ATR-dynamic TP and SL, a redesigned SPS filter with shorter lookback (default 8 candles vs v2's 20), all days active with no day-of-week filters, and a three-tier optimization protocol (Tier 1: 80 combos for ORB structure; Tier 2: 64 combos for SPS and breakeven; Tier 3: 9 combos for session hour and ATR period). The person then asked whether the breakeven trigger should be ATR-controlled rather than percentage-of-TP based, which Claude confirmed as the superior approach. This prompted a v3.1 update (ORB_BreakoutEA_v3_1.mq5) replacing `InpBE_TriggerPct` with `InpBE_ATR_Mult`, where the trigger distance is reconstructed from the position's existing SL using the formula `atr_implied = slDist / InpSL_ATR_Mult`, creating a fully coherent system where TP, SL, and BE all operate in the same ATR units. The person confirmed they will run Tier 1 + Tier 2 optimization (144 total combinations) rather than all parameters simultaneously, with Claude explaining that optimizing all 46,080 combinations simultaneously creates severe data mining bias. Key domain terminology used throughout: IS (in-sample/backtest), OOS (out-of-sample/forward test), PF (profit factor), DD (drawdown), BE (breakeven), SPS (Session Pressure Score), ORB (Opening Range Breakout), RR (risk-reward ratio), prop firm/fondeo (funded trading account challenge).

attachments.extracted_content:
**ORB Breakout EA вҖ” VersiГіn 2**

Resumen de Contexto para ContinuaciГіn de Desarrollo

Jose's Trading Suite  |  Abril 2026  |  MQL5 / MT5  |  XAUUSD M5

# **1. Cambios Principales v1 вҶ’ v2**

| **вң… Lo que se aГұadiГі en v2** + Filtro SPS (Session Pressure Score) вҖ” filtro de tendencia intradГӯa integrado + InpAllowFriday вҖ” faltaba en v1, ahora todos los dГӯas tienen su parГЎmetro + Panel visual muestra seГұal SPS en tiempo real (вҶ‘ Alcista / вҶ“ Bajista / вҶ” Neutral) + Log mejorado: muestra decisiГіn de Гіrdenes con contexto SPS = Arquitectura unificada: todo en un solo .mq5, sin archivos externos (.mqh) |
| --- |

# **2. Filtro SPS вҖ” Session Pressure Score**

## **Concepto**

El SPS mide la presiГіn direccional acumulada de las velas M5 anteriores al cierre del rango ORB. En lugar de usar MAs en timeframes altos (que son lentos y retrospectivos), analiza lo que el mercado estГЎ haciendo ahora mismo en el mismo dГӯa, antes del breakout.

## **Algoritmo**

Para cada vela M5 en la ventana pre-rango:

  body_ratio  = |close - open| / (high - low)    вҶҗ calidad de la vela

  direction   = +1 si alcista,  -1 si bajista

  weight      = (N - idx) / N                     вҶҗ recencia (i=0 mГЎs reciente)

  SPS = ОЈ(direction Г— body_ratio Г— weight) / ОЈ(weight)

  Resultado: score normalizado en [-1.0 , +1.0]

## **InterpretaciГіn del score**

| **Score** | **SeГұal** | **AcciГіn del EA** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| вүҘ +threshold | **SPS_BULLISH  вҶ‘** | Solo BUY STOP (omite SELL) |
| вүӨ -threshold | **SPS_BEARISH  вҶ“** | Solo SELL STOP (omite BUY) |
| Entre Вұthresh | **SPS_NEUTRAL  вҶ”** | Ambas Гіrdenes (ORB normal) |

## **Propiedades clave del SPS**

- 100% intradГӯa: solo usa datos del dГӯa actual (no contamina con dГӯas anteriores)

- No reduce trades agresivamente: en dГӯas neutros opera ambas Гіrdenes normalmente

- Sin indicadores externos: no requiere handles ni llamadas costosas

- Reutilizable: la funciГіn SPS_Calculate() funciona en cualquier EA futuro

- PonderaciГіn por recencia: velas mГЎs cercanas al rango pesan mГЎs

# **3. ParГЎmetros del Filtro SPS**

| **ParГЎmetro** | **Default** | **DescripciГіn** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| InpSPS_Enabled | **true** | Activar/desactivar el filtro completo |
| InpSPS_LookbackCandles | **12** | Velas M5 analizadas (12 = 1 hora antes del rango) |
| InpSPS_Threshold | **0.25** | Umbral de seГұal [0.0-1.0]. Mayor = mГЎs selectivo |
| InpSPS_UseRecencyBias | **true** | Ponderar velas mГЎs recientes con mayor peso |
| InpSPS_PrintDebug | **false** | Imprimir score numГ©rico en el log del EA |

## **Rangos para la primera optimizaciГіn del SPS**

| **InpSPS_LookbackCandles** | **6, 12, 18, 24  (step 6)** |
| --- | --- |
| **InpSPS_Threshold** | **0.15, 0.25, 0.35, 0.45  (step 0.10)** |
| **InpSPS_UseRecencyBias** | **true / false** |
| **Combinaciones totales** | **4 Г— 4 Г— 2 = 32  (muy manejable)** |

# **4. ValidaciГіn IS / OOS (3 configuraciones testeadas)**

## **Tabla comparativa**

| **Test** | **Trades** | **WR** | **PF** | **Net$** | **DD%** | **Ret%** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **BT1 (IS)** | 146 | 48.6% | 1.609 | $+2,285 | -5.2% | +42.5% |
| **BT2 (IS)** | 277 | 44.4% | 1.118 | $+910 | -12.0% | +11.3% |
| **BT3 (IS)** | 217 | 54.4% | 1.022 | $+105 | -10.9% | -1.8% |
| **FT1 (OOS)** | 167 | 37.1% | 1.004 | $+20 | -15.4% | -0.3% |
| **FT2 (OOS)** | 274 | 45.6% | 1.174 | $+1,300 | -10.4% | +21.5% |
| **FT3 (OOS)** | 221 | 61.1% | 1.346 | $+1,456 | -7.5% | +27.0% |

## **AnГЎlisis de degradaciГіn IS вҶ’ OOS**

| **Config 1 (ratio OOS/IS)** | **0.62  рҹҡЁ OVERFITTING  (IS demasiado alto con 146 trades)** |
| --- | --- |
| **Config 2 (ratio OOS/IS)** | **1.05  вң… ROBUSTO  (OOS supera al IS)** |
| **Config 3 (ratio OOS/IS)** | **1.32  вң… ROBUSTO  (OOS supera significativamente al IS)** |
| **Promedio OOS/IS** | **0.94  вң…  (****>****0.70 = robusto en la industria)** |
| **Test chiВІ Win Rate** | **pвүҘ0.05 en todas las configs (WR estadГӯsticamente estable)** |
| **t-test EVвү 0 (OOS)** | **p=0.0577  вҡ пёҸ  Casi significativo вҖ” necesita mГЎs volumen** |

| **вң… Veredicto IS/OOS: VIABLE con matices** PF > 1.0 en los 3 tests OOS  вң… Net positivo en todos los tests OOS  вң… DD < 20% en los 3 tests OOS  вң… Ratio OOS/IS promedio = 0.94  вң…  (excelente retenciГіn de borde) Config 1 muestra overfitting вҖ” 146 trades IS es muestra pequeГұa con SL/TP extremos Configs 2 y 3 son genuinamente robustas вҖ” OOS mejora al IS en ambas |
| --- |

# **5. Archivo MQL5**

| **Nombre** | **ORB_BreakoutEA_v2.mq5** |
| --- | --- |
| **Carpeta** | **MQL5/Experts/** |
| **VersiГіn** | **2.00** |
| **Magic #** | **20260401** |
| **Dependencias** | **<****Trade\Trade.mqh****>****  Гәnicamente вҖ” no requiere archivos externos** |
| **Nota** | **El SPS estГЎ integrado directamente en el .mq5 (no usa .mqh externo)** |

# **6. PrГіximos Pasos Sugeridos**

- Optimizar los 3 parГЎmetros SPS en la configuraciГіn 2 o 3 (las mГЎs robustas)

- Correr walk-forward con SPS activo: IS 2024, OOS 2025-2026

- Comparar equity curve con SPS=ON vs SPS=OFF para cuantificar el impacto

- Evaluar si el filtro de dГӯa Jueves sigue siendo necesario con SPS activo

- Analizar BUY vs SELL dentro de cada seГұal SPS para detectar asimetrГӯas

- Considerar aГұadir trailing stop como alternativa al BE fijo

| **рҹ“Ң ParГЎmetros recomendados para continuar (Config 3 como base)** InpRangeCandles = 5    в”Ӯ   InpSessionHour = 17   в”Ӯ   InpSessionMinute = 0 InpTP_Pips = 60        в”Ӯ   InpSL_Pips = 70       в”Ӯ   InpBE_Pips = 0 InpSPS_Enabled = true  в”Ӯ   InpSPS_Threshold = 0.25  в”Ӯ  InpSPS_LookbackCandles = 12 InpAllowThursday = false  (dato consistente en 4 aГұos de IS) |
| --- |

Este documento es la base de contexto para continuar el desarrollo en el siguiente chat.

There's a discrepancy between the MT5 strategy tester results (showing 44-45% for v1) and my computed win rates from the actual deals. The MT5 report might be counting profitable positions differentlyвҖ”possibly treating break-even trades or canceled orders differently than how I'm calculating from the deal data.

Looking at the MT5 report numbers more closely, I see the issue: MT5 counts each order separately (both entry and exit), so 236 trades generate 472 transactions. The 106 profitable positions MT5 reports versus my 89 winsвҖ”that 17-trade difference matches exactly with my break-even count, meaning MT5 is including break-even trades as profitable while I'm counting them separately.

For prop firm evaluation, break-even trades don't really matter since they don't impact P&L, so what matters is hitting the daily drawdown limit (usually 4-5%), total drawdown (8-10%), reaching the net profit target (8-10%), and maintaining consistency without blowing up any single period.

Looking at the year-by-year breakdown across all versions, v1 SET1 and SET2 both show consistent profitability in backtesting and forward testing periods, with SET2 forward testing particularly strong at +$2856 in 2025. v2 SET1 is just starting to show results. Looking at the rest of v2 SET3 FR results and the forward testing data, I'm noticing a clear pattern in how these strategies perform out-of-sample. The backtesting results look strong across the board, but when I compare them to forward testing, there's some degradation in the profit factorsвҖ”v2 SET1 shows a concerning 18% drop, while v1 SET2 actually improved, which is interesting.

The win rates tell an even starker story. V1 holds steady or improves slightly in forward testing, but v2 shows consistent deterioration across all three sets, with SET3 dropping from 22.5% down to 18.2%вҖ”that's a significant decline that suggests potential overfitting in the v2 models.

Now I'm checking these results against prop firm requirements. Most firms like FundingPips enforce strict drawdown limitsвҖ”typically 4-5% daily and 8-10% totalвҖ”with profit targets in that same 8-10% range. Looking at the maximum drawdown metrics, v1 SET1 is already pushing it at 11.82% on balance, which exceeds the 8% threshold, and the equity drawdown of 12.77% is even worse. V1 SET2 follows a similar pattern with 11.66% balance drawdown. Even the forward testing results for v1 are marginal at best, sitting right at or slightly above the acceptable limits.

V2 shows more promise in certain configurations. V2 SET1 on the backtest hits 8.27% balance drawdown with 8.57% equityвҖ”just barely within tolerance. V2 SET2 backtest is cleaner at 8.13% balance and 8.43% equity. But the forward results are inconsistent; V2 SET1 FR spikes to 10.76% equity drawdown, and V2 SET3 FR is completely unacceptable at 14.16% balance and 12.68% equity. The pattern emerging is that v1 SET2 appears to be the most stable across both backtest and forward periods, maintaining more consistent performance without the wild swings I'm seeing in the v2 variants.

The core issue is that v2's lower win ratesвҖ”sitting between 18-28%вҖ”create psychological strain and expose the strategy to brutal losing streaks that prop firms won't tolerate. V1's 35-39% win rate is far more sustainable even if the reward-to-risk ratios aren't as sharp. V2 compensates with better risk-reward (2.42-2.65 versus v1's 1.51-2.17), but that advantage evaporates when the strategy can't maintain consistency in live trading.

The break-even mechanism in v2 is also working against itвҖ”SET3 FR shows nearly 20% of trades hitting break-even, which suggests the BE_pips threshold is too tight and stops out positions before they can reach target. For a prop firm challenge, v1 SET2 FR looks like the safer bet if I strip out the Thursday filter and dial back position sizing to keep drawdown under control.

## Veredicto principal: v1.1 SET 2 gana para prop firm

El dashboard te lo dice directo: **v1.1 SET 2 en forward testing es la configuraciГіn mГЎs robusta**. El Гәnico set donde el OOS supera al IS con ratio 1.18 вҖ” eso es la seГұal mГЎs limpia de borde genuino que puedes tener. v2 se ve mejor en backtesting, pero se desintegra en forward.

---

## AnГЎlisis profundo: ВҝquГ© estГЎ pasando?

**Por quГ© v1.1 SET 2 gana:**
El sesiГіn hour=17, range=4 velas, TP=130, SL=60 sin BE es una combinaciГіn que no necesita condiciones especiales para funcionar. En BK tiene PF=1.141, lo cual parece mediocre, pero en FR sube a 1.343 вҖ” eso es contracorriente y extremadamente raro. Win rate tambiГ©n sube del 35.1% al 38.9% en OOS. Cada mГ©trica mejora fuera de muestra, lo que indica que el borde es real y no era producto de datos vistos.

**Por quГ© v2 falla en OOS:**
El filtro SPS aprende del comportamiento intradГӯa del mismo perГӯodo que se optimizГі. En IS con lookback=20 velas y threshold=0.25, el score se calibra bien porque el perГӯodo de entrenamiento tiene ciertos patrones. En OOS esos patrones no se repiten igual, el filtro descarta trades que hubieran ganado y pasa trades que pierden. El resultado: win rate OOS del 18-24% vs 22-28% IS. TambiГ©n el TP de 395 pips en XAUUSD M5 es un objetivo que pocas velas de una sesiГіn alcanzan, lo que genera muchos trades con BE activado (hasta 19% de los trades en v2 SET 3 FR terminan en cero).

**El problema del BE alto en v2:** Con BE activado a 225-260 pips, el precio primero tiene que moverse 225 pips para activar el BE, y luego el trailing natural del mercado toca el BE y cierra. Muchos de esos trades hubieran seguido hasta el TP de 395 pips si no existiera el BE. En v1 SET 2 directamente no hay BE вҖ” el trade vive o muere por SL/TP puro. Eso es mГЎs limpio.

**Win rate real vs reporte MT5:** Los nГәmeros del MT5 (44-45%) incluyen los trades BE como "rentables" porque terminaron en equilibrio o con comisiГіn mГӯnima. El win rate real (solo trades con profit > 0) es 35-39% para v1 y 18-28% para v2. Para prop firms ese WR real importa porque los streaks de pГ©rdidas consecutivas son lo que mata psicolГіgicamente y genera errores.

---

## Problema crГӯtico compartido: el DD

Ninguna configuraciГіn pasa limpiamente el lГӯmite de 8-10% de equity DD de FundingPips en forward testing. El mejor es v2 SET 1 BK con 8.57% equity вҖ” pero eso es IS, no la realidad. En FR el equity DD sube a 10.76%. La soluciГіn no es cambiar la estrategia: es reducir el lot sizing un 20-30% para que el mismo edge pase con DD controlado.

Con una reducciГіn de tamaГұo de posiciГіn del 25%, v1 SET 2 FR tendrГӯa:
- DD mГЎximo equity: ~7.0% (dentro del lГӯmite)
- Net profit OOS: ~$2,133 en un aГұo вҶ’ retorno ~42% вҶ’ sigue siendo mГЎs que suficiente para pasar el challenge

---

## Pros y contras

**v1.1 SET 2**
- Pros: OOS genuinamente robusto, WR psicolГіgicamente manejable, RR=2.17, sin BE que complique la lГіgica, sesiГіn NY 17:00 es hora de mayor liquidez en XAUUSD, parГЎmetros simples
- Contras: DD en IS supera 10%, necesita reducciГіn de lot para prop firm, solo un trade por dГӯa limita la frecuencia

**v2 (general)**
- Pros: RR excelente (2.42-2.65), DD en IS mejor controlado, concepto SPS es sГіlido en teorГӯa, mayor net profit en IS
- Contras: DegradaciГіn OOS sistemГЎtica en todos los sets, win rate OOS demasiado bajo (peligroso para prop firms con streak limits), TP de 395 pips irrealista para M5, BE demasiado temprano genera muchos trades a cero, Thursday inconsistente

---

## Propuesta para v3.0

BasГЎndome en lo que funcionГі y lo que no:

**Estructura base: v1.1 SET 2 (hora 17, range=4, TP=130, SL=60)**

1. **SPS rediseГұado** вҖ” si se mantiene, usar lookback mГЎs corto (6-8 velas = 30-40 min) y threshold mГЎs agresivo (0.35+) para que filtre menos y solo bloquee seГұales muy claras contra tendencia. El lookback de 20 velas (100 minutos) es demasiado largo y no captura el momentum inmediato al breakout.

2. **Thursday=false** вҖ” el documento v2 lo recomienda y los datos histГіricos son consistentes. Aplicarlo en v3 desde el primer test.

3. **BE adaptativo o sin BE** вҖ” si se usa BE, ponerlo a 50-60% del TP (65-78 pips con TP=130), no al 85-90% como en v2. O simplemente eliminarlo como en v1 SET 2.

4. **TP dinГЎmico por ATR** вҖ” en lugar de TP fijo de 130 pips, usar ATR(14) x 1.5 como TP. En dГӯas de alta volatilidad el target se expande, en dГӯas tranquilos se contrae. Reduce el riesgo de que el TP fijo nunca se alcance en sesiones de rango comprimido.

5. **TamaГұo de posiciГіn basado en ATR** вҖ” SL en pips = ATR actual, no fijo. Con SL dinГЎmico el riesgo por trade es constante en tГ©rminos de volatilidad del mercado, no en pips arbitrarios.

6. **Filtro de dГӯa (expansiГіn de datos)** вҖ” correr anГЎlisis por dГӯa de la semana con los datos que tienes y determinar estadГӯsticamente cuГЎles son negativos. El documento ya muestra Jueves como consistentemente malo. Puede haber otros patrones por descubrir.

7. **ValidaciГіn IS 2022-2023 + OOS 2024-2026** вҖ” el backtesting actual solo usa 2024-2026. Para v3 necesitas al menos 3-4 aГұos de IS para que el modelo sea mГЎs robusto. 2022 y 2023 fueron aГұos muy diferentes para el oro (subidas de tasas Fed, reversiones bruscas).

El objetivo de v3 deberГӯa ser llegar a un forward test con PF вүҘ 1.25 y DD equity < 7% con lot sizing estГЎndar. Con eso, un challenge de FundingPips con 8% target y 8% max DD se puede pasar en 60-90 dГӯas de operaciГіn.

content.message: Reading backtest expert skill

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: backtest-expert
     3	description: Expert guidance for systematic backtesting of trading strategies. Use when developing, testing, stress-testing, or validating quantitative trading strategies. Covers "beating ideas to death" methodology, parameter robustness testing, slippage modeling, bias prevention, and interpreting backtest results. Applicable when user asks about backtesting, strategy validation, robustness testing, avoiding overfitting, or systematic trading development.
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Backtest Expert
     7	
     8	Systematic approach to backtesting trading strategies based on professional methodology that prioritizes robustness over optimistic results.
     9	
    10	## Core Philosophy
    11	
    12	**Goal**: Find strategies that "break the least", not strategies that "profit the most" on paper.
    13	
    14	**Principle**: Add friction, stress test assumptions, and see what survives. If a strategy holds up under pessimistic conditions, it's more likely to work in live trading.
    15	
    16	## When to Use This Skill
    17	
    18	Use this skill when:
    19	- Developing or validating systematic trading strategies
    20	- Evaluating whether a trading idea is robust enough for live implementation  
    21	- Troubleshooting why a backtest might be misleading
    22	- Learning proper backtesting methodology
    23	- Avoiding common pitfalls (curve-fitting, look-ahead bias, survivorship bias)
    24	- Assessing parameter sensitivity and regime dependence
    25	- Setting realistic expectations for slippage and execution costs
    26	
    27	## Backtesting Workflow
    28	
    29	### 1. State the Hypothesis
    30	
    31	Define the edge in one sentence.
    32	
    33	**Example**: "Stocks that gap up >3% on earnings and pull back to previous day's close within first hour provide mean-reversion opportunity."
    34	
    35	If you can't articulate the edge clearly, don't proceed to testing.
    36	
    37	### 2. Codify Rules with Zero Discretion
    38	
    39	Define with complete specificity:
    40	- **Entry**: Exact conditions, timing, price type
    41	- **Exit**: Stop loss, profit target, time-based exit
    42	- **Position sizing**: Fixed $$, % of portfolio, volatility-adjusted
    43	- **Filters**: Market cap, volume, sector, volatility conditions
    44	- **Universe**: What instruments are eligible
    45	
    46	**Critical**: No subjective judgment allowed. Every decision must be rule-based and unambiguous.
    47	
    48	### 3. Run Initial Backtest
    49	
    50	Test over:
    51	- **Minimum 5 years** (preferably 10+)
    52	- **Multiple market regimes** (bull, bear, high/low volatility)
    53	- **Realistic costs**: Commissions + conservative slippage
    54	
    55	Examine initial results for basic viability. If fundamentally broken, iterate on hypothesis.
    56	
    57	### 4. Stress Test the Strategy
    58	
    59	This is where 80% of testing time should be spent.
    60	
    61	**Parameter sensitivity**:
    62	- Test stop loss at 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, 150% of baseline
    63	- Test profit target at 80%, 90%, 100%, 110%, 120% of baseline  
    64	- Vary entry/exit timing by Вұ15-30 minutes
    65	- Look for "plateaus" of stable performance, not narrow spikes
    66	
    67	**Execution friction**:
    68	- Increase slippage to 1.5-2x typical estimates
    69	- Model worst-case fills (buy at ask+1 tick, sell at bid-1 tick)
    70	- Add realistic order rejection scenarios
    71	- Test with pessimistic commission structures
    72	
    73	**Time robustness**:
    74	- Analyze year-by-year performance
    75	- Require positive expectancy in majority of years
    76	- Ensure strategy doesn't rely on 1-2 exceptional periods
    77	- Test in different market regimes separately
    78	
    79	**Sample size**:
    80	- Absolute minimum: 30 trades
    81	- Preferred: 100+ trades
    82	- High confidence: 200+ trades
    83	
    84	### 5. Out-of-Sample Validation
    85	
    86	**Walk-forward analysis**:
    87	1. Optimize on training period (e.g., Year 1-3)
    88	2. Test on validation period (Year 4)
    89	3. Roll forward and repeat
    90	4. Compare in-sample vs out-of-sample performance
    91	
    92	**Warning signs**:
    93	- Out-of-sample <50% of in-sample performance
    94	- Need frequent parameter re-optimization
    95	- Parameters change dramatically between periods
    96	
    97	### 6. Evaluate Results
    98	
    99	**Questions to answer**:
   100	- Does edge survive pessimistic assumptions?
   101	- Is performance stable across parameter variations?
   102	- Does strategy work in multiple market regimes?
   103	- Is sample size sufficient for statistical confidence?
   104	- Are results realistic, not "too good to be true"?
   105	
   106	**Decision criteria**:
   107	- вң… **Deploy**: Survives all stress tests with acceptable performance
   108	- рҹ”„ **Refine**: Core logic sound but needs parameter adjustment
   109	- вқҢ **Abandon**: Fails stress tests or relies on fragile assumptions
   110	
   111	## Key Testing Principles
   112	
   113	### Punish the Strategy
   114	
   115	Add friction everywhere:
   116	- Commissions higher than reality
   117	- Slippage 1.5-2x typical
   118	- Worst-case fills
   119	- Order rejections
   120	- Partial fills
   121	
   122	**Rationale**: Strategies that survive pessimistic assumptions often outperform in live trading.
   123	
   124	### Seek Plateaus, Not Peaks
   125	
   126	Look for parameter ranges where performance is stable, not optimal values that create performance spikes.
   127	
   128	**Good**: Strategy profitable with stop loss anywhere from 1.5% to 3.0%
   129	**Bad**: Strategy only works with stop loss at exactly 2.13%
   130	
   131	Stable performance indicates genuine edge; narrow optima suggest curve-fitting.
   132	
   133	### Test All Cases, Not Cherry-Picked Examples
   134	
   135	**Wrong approach**: Study hand-picked "market leaders" that worked
   136	**Right approach**: Test every stock that met criteria, including those that failed
   137	
   138	Selective examples create survivorship bias and overestimate strategy quality.
   139	
   140	### Separate Idea Generation from Validation
   141	
   142	**Intuition**: Useful for generating hypotheses
   143	**Validation**: Must be purely data-driven
   144	
   145	Never let attachment to an idea influence interpretation of test results.
   146	
   147	## Common Failure Patterns
   148	
   149	Recognize these patterns early to save time:
   150	
   151	1. **Parameter sensitivity**: Only works with exact parameter values
   152	2. **Regime-specific**: Great in some years, terrible in others  
   153	3. **Slippage sensitivity**: Unprofitable when realistic costs added
   154	4. **Small sample**: Too few trades for statistical confidence
   155	5. **Look-ahead bias**: "Too good to be true" results
   156	6. **Over-optimization**: Many parameters, poor out-of-sample results
   157	
   158	See `references/failed_tests.md` for detailed examples and diagnostic framework.
   159	
   160	## Available Reference Documentation
   161	
   162	### Methodology Reference
   163	**File**: `references/methodology.md`
   164	
   165	**When to read**: For detailed guidance on specific testing techniques.
   166	
   167	**Contents**:
   168	- Stress testing methods
   169	- Parameter sensitivity analysis  
   170	- Slippage and friction modeling
   171	- Sample size requirements
   172	- Market regime classification
   173	- Common biases and pitfalls (survivorship, look-ahead, curve-fitting, etc.)
   174	
   175	### Failed Tests Reference
   176	**File**: `references/failed_tests.md`
   177	
   178	**When to read**: When strategy fails tests, or learning from past mistakes.
   179	
   180	**Contents**:
   181	- Why failures are valuable
   182	- Common failure patterns with examples
   183	- Case study documentation framework
   184	- Red flags checklist for evaluating backtests
   185	
   186	## Critical Reminders
   187	
   188	**Time allocation**: Spend 20% generating ideas, 80% trying to break them.
   189	
   190	**Context-free requirement**: If strategy requires "perfect context" to work, it's not robust enough for systematic trading.
   191	
   192	**Red flag**: If backtest results look too good (>90% win rate, minimal drawdowns, perfect timing), audit carefully for look-ahead bias or data issues.
   193	
   194	**Tool limitations**: Understand your backtesting platform's quirks (interpolation methods, handling of low liquidity, data alignment issues).
   195	
   196	**Statistical significance**: Small edges require large sample sizes to prove. 5% edge per trade needs 100+ trades to distinguish from luck.
   197	
   198	## Discretionary vs Systematic Differences
   199	
   200	This skill focuses on **systematic/quantitative** backtesting where:
   201	- All rules are codified in advance
   202	- No discretion or "feel" in execution  
   203	- Testing happens on all historical examples, not cherry-picked cases
   204	- Context (news, macro) is deliberately stripped out
   205	
   206	Discretionary traders study differentlyвҖ”this skill may not apply to setups requiring subjective judgment.
   207

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: file-reading
     3	description: "Use this skill when a file has been uploaded but its content is NOT in your context вҖ” only its path at /mnt/user-data/uploads/ is listed in an uploaded_files block. This skill is a router: it tells you which tool to use for each file type (pdf, docx, xlsx, csv, json, images, archives, ebooks) so you read the right amount the right way instead of blindly running cat on a binary. Triggers: any mention of /mnt/user-data/uploads/, an uploaded_files section, a file_path tag, or a user asking about an uploaded file you have not yet read. Do NOT use this skill if the file content is already visible in your context inside a documents block вҖ” you already have it."
     4	compatibility: "claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Cowork вҖ” any surface where uploads land at /mnt/user-data/uploads/"
     5	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     6	---
     7	
     8	# Reading Uploaded Files
     9	
    10	## Why this skill exists
    11	
    12	When a user uploads a file in claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Cowork,
    13	the file is written to `/mnt/user-data/uploads/<filename>` and you are told the path
    14	in an `<uploaded_files>` block. **The content is not in your context.**
    15	You must go read it.
    16	
    17	The naive thing вҖ” `cat /mnt/user-data/uploads/whatever` вҖ” is wrong for
    18	most files:
    19	
    20	- On a PDF it prints binary garbage.
    21	- On a 100MB CSV it floods your context with rows you will never use.
    22	- On a DOCX it prints the raw ZIP bytes.
    23	- On an image it does nothing useful at all.
    24	
    25	This skill tells you the right first move for each type, and when to
    26	hand off to a deeper skill.
    27	
    28	## General protocol
    29	
    30	1. **Look at the extension.** That is your dispatch key.
    31	2. **Stat before you read.** Large files need sampling, not slurping.
    32	   ```bash
    33	   stat -c '%s bytes, %y' /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    34	   file /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    35	   ```
    36	3. **Read just enough to answer the user's question.** If they asked
    37	   "how many rows are in this CSV", don't load the whole thing into
    38	   pandas вҖ” `wc -l` gives a fast approximation (it counts newlines,
    39	   not CSV records, so it may over-count if quoted fields contain
    40	   embedded newlines).
    41	4. **If a dedicated skill exists, go read it.** The table below tells
    42	   you when. The dedicated skills cover editing, creating, and advanced
    43	   operations that this skill does not.
    44	
    45	## Dispatch table
    46	
    47	| Extension                         | First move                                           | Dedicated skill                           |
    48	| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
    49	| `.pdf`                            | Content inventory (see PDF section)                  | `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` |
    50	| `.docx`                           | `pandoc` to markdown                                 | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    51	| `.doc` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.docx` first вҖ” pandoc cannot read it     | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    52	| `.xlsx`, `.xlsm`                  | `openpyxl` sheet names + head                        | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    53	| `.xls` (legacy)                   | `pd.read_excel(engine="xlrd")` вҖ” openpyxl rejects it | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    54	| `.ods`                            | `pd.read_excel(engine="odf")` вҖ” openpyxl rejects it  | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    55	| `.pptx`                           | `python-pptx` slide count                            | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    56	| `.ppt` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.pptx` first вҖ” python-pptx rejects it    | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    57	| `.csv`, `.tsv`                    | `pandas` with `nrows`                                | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    58	| `.json`, `.jsonl`                 | `jq` for structure                                   | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    59	| `.jpg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.webp`   | Already in your context as vision input              | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    60	| `.zip`, `.tar`, `.tar.gz`         | List contents, do **not** auto-extract               | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    61	| `.gz` (single file)               | `zcat \| head` вҖ” no manifest to list                 | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    62	| `.epub`, `.odt`                   | `pandoc` to plain text                               | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    63	| `.rtf`                            | `pandoc` (needs 3.1.7+) or soffice via docx skill    | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    64	| `.txt`, `.md`, `.log`, code files | `wc -c` then `head` or full `cat`                    | вҖ” (below)                                 |
    65	| Unknown                           | `file` then decide                                   | вҖ”                                         |
    66	
    67	---
    68	
    69	## PDF
    70	
    71	**Never** `cat` a PDF вҖ” it prints binary garbage.
    72	
    73	Quick first move вҖ” get the page count and check if text is extractable:
    74	
    75	```bash
    76	pdfinfo /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    77	pdftotext -f 1 -l 1 /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf - | head -20
    78	```
    79	
    80	Then peek at the text content:
    81	
    82	```python
    83	from pypdf import PdfReader
    84	r = PdfReader("/mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf")
    85	print(f"{len(r.pages)} pages")
    86	print(r.pages[0].extract_text()[:2000])
    87	```
    88	
    89	For anything beyond a quick peek вҖ” figures, tables, attachments,
    90	forms, scanned PDFs, visual inspection, or choosing a reading strategy
    91	вҖ” go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md`. It covers
    92	content inventory, text extraction vs. page rasterization, embedded
    93	content extraction, and document-type-aware reading strategies.
    94	
    95	For PDF form filling, creation, merging, splitting, or watermarking,
    96	go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.
    97	
    98	---
    99	
   100	## DOCX / DOC
   101	
   102	The `docx` skill covers editing, creating, tracked changes, images.
   103	Read it if you need any of those. For a quick look:
   104	
   105	```bash
   106	pandoc /mnt/user-data/uploads/memo.docx -t markdown | head -200
   107	```
   108	
   109	Legacy `.doc` (not `.docx`) must be converted first вҖ” see the `docx`
   110	skill.
   111	
   112	---
   113	
   114	## XLSX / XLS / spreadsheets
   115	
   116	The `xlsx` skill covers formulas, formatting, charts, creating. Read
   117	it if you need any of those. For a quick look at `.xlsx` / `.xlsm`:
   118	
   119	```python
   120	from openpyxl import load_workbook
   121	wb = load_workbook("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx", read_only=True)
   122	print("Sheets:", wb.sheetnames)
   123	ws = wb.active
   124	for row in ws.iter_rows(max_row=5, values_only=True):
   125	    print(row)
   126	```
   127	
   128	`read_only=True` matters вҖ” without it, openpyxl loads the entire
   129	workbook into memory, which breaks on large files. Do not trust
   130	`ws.max_row` in read-only mode: many non-Excel writers omit the
   131	dimension record, so it comes back `None` or wrong. If you need a row
   132	count, iterate or use pandas.
   133	
   134	**Legacy `.xls`** вҖ” openpyxl raises `InvalidFileException`. Use:
   135	
   136	```python
   137	import pandas as pd
   138	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/old.xls", engine="xlrd", nrows=5)
   139	```
   140	
   141	**`.ods` (OpenDocument)** вҖ” openpyxl also rejects this. Use:
   142	
   143	```python
   144	import pandas as pd
   145	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.ods", engine="odf", nrows=5)
   146	```
   147	
   148	---
   149	
   150	## PPTX
   151	
   152	```python
   153	from itertools import islice
   154	from pptx import Presentation
   155	p = Presentation("/mnt/user-data/uploads/deck.pptx")
   156	print(f"{len(p.slides)} slides")
   157	for i, slide in enumerate(islice(p.slides, 3), 1):
   158	    texts = [s.text for s in slide.shapes if s.has_text_frame]
   159	    print(f"Slide {i}:", " | ".join(t for t in texts if t))
   160	```
   161	
   162	`p.slides` is not subscriptable вҖ” `p.slides[:3]` raises
   163	`AttributeError`. Use `islice` or `list(p.slides)[:3]`.
   164	
   165	**Legacy `.ppt`** вҖ” python-pptx only reads OOXML. Convert to `.pptx`
   166	first via LibreOffice; see `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md` for the
   167	sandbox-safe `scripts/office/soffice.py` wrapper (bare `soffice` hangs
   168	here because the seccomp filter blocks the `AF_UNIX` sockets
   169	LibreOffice uses for instance management).
   170	
   171	For anything beyond reading, go to `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`.
   172	
   173	---
   174	
   175	## CSV / TSV
   176	
   177	**Do not** `cat` or `head` these blindly. A CSV with a 50KB quoted cell
   178	in row 1 will wreck your `head -5`. Use pandas with `nrows`:
   179	
   180	```python
   181	import pandas as pd
   182	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv", nrows=5)
   183	print(df)
   184	print()
   185	print(df.dtypes)
   186	```
   187	
   188	Approximate row count without loading (over-counts if the file has
   189	RFC-4180 quoted newlines вҖ” the same quoted-cell case this section
   190	warned about above):
   191	
   192	```bash
   193	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv
   194	```
   195	
   196	Full analysis only after you know the shape:
   197	
   198	```python
   199	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv")
   200	print(df.describe())
   201	```
   202	
   203	TSV: same, with `sep="\t"`.
   204	
   205	---
   206	
   207	## JSON / JSONL
   208	
   209	Structure first, content second:
   210	
   211	```bash
   212	jq 'type' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   213	jq 'if type == "array" then length elif type == "object" then keys else . end' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   214	```
   215	
   216	(`keys` errors on scalar JSON roots вҖ” a bare `"hello"` or `42` is valid
   217	JSON per RFC 7159 вҖ” so guard the branch.)
   218	
   219	Then drill into what the user actually asked about.
   220	
   221	JSONL (one object per line) вҖ” do **not** `jq` the whole file; work line
   222	by line:
   223	
   224	```bash
   225	head -3 /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl | jq .
   226	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl
   227	```
   228	
   229	---
   230	
   231	## Images (JPG / PNG / GIF / WEBP)
   232	
   233	**You can already see uploaded images.** They are injected into your
   234	context as vision inputs alongside the `<uploaded_files>` pointer. You
   235	do not need to read them from disk to describe them.
   236	
   237	The disk copy is only needed if you are going to **process** the image
   238	programmatically:
   239	
   240	```python
   241	from PIL import Image
   242	img = Image.open("/mnt/user-data/uploads/photo.jpg")
   243	print(img.size, img.mode, img.format)
   244	```
   245	
   246	For OCR on an image (text extraction, not description):
   247	
   248	```python
   249	import pytesseract
   250	print(pytesseract.image_to_string(img))
   251	```
   252	
   253	Note: the client resizes images larger than 2000Г—2000 down to that
   254	bound and re-encodes as JPEG before upload, so the disk copy may not
   255	be the user's original bytes. For most processing this doesn't matter;
   256	if the user is asking about original-resolution pixel data, flag it.
   257	
   258	---
   259	
   260	## Archives (ZIP / TAR / TAR.GZ)
   261	
   262	**List first. Extract never вҖ” unless the user explicitly asks.**
   263	Archives can be huge, contain path traversal, or nest forever.
   264	
   265	```bash
   266	unzip -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip
   267	tar -tf /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.tar
   268	```
   269	
   270	GNU tar auto-detects compression вҖ” `tar -tf` works on `.tar`,
   271	`.tar.gz`, `.tar.bz2`, `.tar.xz` alike. Don't hard-code `-z`.
   272	
   273	If the user wants one file from inside, extract just that one:
   274	
   275	```bash
   276	unzip -p /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip path/inside/file.txt
   277	```
   278	
   279	**Standalone `.gz`** (not a tar) compresses a single file вҖ” there is
   280	no manifest to list. Just peek at the decompressed content:
   281	
   282	```bash
   283	zcat /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json.gz | head -50
   284	```
   285	
   286	---
   287	
   288	## EPUB / ODT
   289	
   290	```bash
   291	pandoc /mnt/user-data/uploads/book.epub -t plain | head -200
   292	```
   293	
   294	For long ebooks, pipe through `head` вҖ” you rarely need the whole thing
   295	to answer a question.
   296	
   297	---
   298	
   299	## RTF
   300	
   301	Pandoc's RTF reader was added in 3.1.7 (Oct 2023). Debian Bookworm
   302	ships 2.17, so try pandoc first but expect it may fail:
   303	
   304	```bash
   305	pandoc /mnt/user-data/uploads/notes.rtf -t plain | head -200
   306	```
   307	
   308	If you see `Unknown input format rtf`, convert via LibreOffice using
   309	the sandbox-safe wrapper вҖ” see `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md` for
   310	`scripts/office/soffice.py` (do not call bare `soffice`; see the PPTX
   311	section above for why).
   312	
   313	---
   314	
   315	## Plain text / code / logs
   316	
   317	Check the size first:
   318	
   319	```bash
   320	wc -c /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   321	```
   322	
   323	- **Under ~20KB**: `cat` is fine.
   324	- **Over ~20KB**: `head -100` and `tail -100` to orient. If the user
   325	  asked about something specific, `grep` for it. Load the whole thing
   326	  only if you genuinely need all of it.
   327	
   328	For log files, the user almost always cares about the end:
   329	
   330	```bash
   331	tail -200 /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   332	```
   333	
   334	---
   335	
   336	## Unknown extension
   337	
   338	```bash
   339	file /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin
   340	xxd /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin | head -5
   341	```
   342	
   343	`file` identifies most things. `xxd` head shows magic bytes. If `file`
   344	says "data" and the hex doesn't match anything you recognize, ask the
   345	user what it is instead of guessing.
   346

input.description: Read all Excel files and explore their structure
input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
from openpyxl import load_workbook

files = [
    "set1_bk", "set1_fr", "set2_bk", "set2_fr",
    "SET_1_BK", "SET_1_FR", "SET_2_BK", "SET_2_FR", "SET_3_BK", "SET_3_FR"
]

for f in files:
    path = f"/mnt/user-data/uploads/{f}.xlsx"
    try:
        wb = load_workbook(path, read_only=True)
        print(f"\n=== {f}.xlsx ===")
        print(f"Sheets: {wb.sheetnames}")
        ws = wb.active
        rows = list(ws.iter_rows(max_row=3, values_only=True))
        for r in rows:
            print(r)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error {f}: {e}")
EOF

{"language": "bash", "code": "python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
from openpyxl import load_workbook

files = [
    "set1_bk", "set1_fr", "set2_bk", "set2_fr",
    "SET_1_BK", "SET_1_FR", "SET_2_BK", "SET_2_FR", "SET_3_BK", "SET_3_FR"
]

for f in files:

        rows = list(ws.iter_rows(max_row=3, values_only=True))
        for r in rows:

        print(f"Error {f}: {e}")

python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd

files = {
    "v1_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_fr.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_fr.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_FR.xlsx",
}

# Extract trades and per-year stats from each file
def get_trades(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    # Find orders section
    trades = []
    in_orders = False
    header_row = None
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        non_null = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not non_null:
            continue
        
        if str(non_null[0]) == 'Г“rdenes':
            in_orders = True
            continue
        
        if in_orders and str(non_null[0]) == 'Hora de apertura':
            header_row = i
            continue
        
        # Parse trade rows - they start with a date
        if in_orders and header_row and len(non_null) >= 6:
            try:
                date_str = str(non_null[0])
                if '.' in date_str and ':' in date_str and len(date_str) > 10:
                    # This looks like a date
                    trade = {
                        'open_time': non_null[0],
                        'type': non_null[3] if len(non_null) > 3 else None,
                        'price': non_null[5] if len(non_null) > 5 else None,
                        'sl': non_null[6] if len(non_null) > 6 else None,
                        'tp': non_null[7] if len(non_null) > 7 else None,
                        'close_time': non_null[8] if len(non_null) > 8 else None,
                        'status': non_null[9] if len(non_null) > 9 else None,
                    }
                    trades.append(trade)
            except:
                pass
    
    return trades

# Extract full row data to find more metrics
def extract_full_metrics(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    metrics = {}
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        non_null = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not non_null:
            continue
        
        row_str = ' '.join([str(v) for v in non_null])
        
        # Get all rows with numbers for debugging
        if i >= 34 and i <= 60:
            print(f"  Row {i}: {non_null}")

for name, path in files.items():
    print(f"\n=== {name} ===")
    extract_full_metrics(path)
    break  # Just check one file

EOF

import pandas as pd

files = {

    "v2_SET3_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_FR.xlsx",
}

# Extract trades and per-year stats from each file
def get_trades(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    # Find orders section
    trades = []
    in_orders = False
    header_row = None
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        non_null = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not non_null:
            continue
        
        if str(non_null[0]) == 'Órdenes':
            in_orders = True
            continue
        
        if in_orders and str(non_null[0]) == 'Hora de apertura':
            header_row = i
            continue
        
        # Parse trade rows - they start with a date
        if in_orders and header_row and len(non_null) >= 6:
            try:
                date_str = str(non_null[0])
                if '.' in date_str and ':' in date_str and len(date_str) > 10:
                    # This looks like a date
                    trade = {
                        'open_time': non_null[0],
                        'type': non_null[3] if len(non_null) > 3 else None,
                        'price': non_null[5] if len(non_null) > 5 else None,
                        'sl': non_null[6] if len(non_null) > 6 else None,
                        'tp': non_null[7] if len(non_null) > 7 else None,
                        'close_time': non_null[8] if len(non_null) > 8 else None,
                        'status': non_null[9] if len(non_null) > 9 else None,
                    }
                    trades.append(trade)
            except:
                pass
    
    return trades

# Extract full row data to find more metrics
def extract_full_metrics(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    metrics = {}
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        non_null = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not non_null:
            continue
        
        row_str = ' '.join([str(v) for v in non_null])
        
        # Get all rows with numbers for debugging
        if i >= 34 and i <= 60:
            print(f"  Row {i}: {non_null}")

for name, path in files.items():
    print(f"\n=== {name} ===")
    extract_full_metrics(path)
    break  # Just check one file

EOF

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import re

files = {
    "v1_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_fr.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_fr.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_FR.xlsx",
}

def extract_pct(s):
    """Extract percentage from strings like '11.82% (809.50)' or '106 (44.92%)'"""
    if not s:
        return None
    s = str(s)
    # Try pattern like '11.82%'
    m = re.search(r'([\d.]+)%', s)
    if m:
        return float(m.group(1))
    return None

def extract_all_metrics(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    m = {}
    trades = []
    in_orders = False
    header_found = False
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        nn = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not nn:
            continue
        
        r0 = str(nn[0])
        
        if 'Experto:' in r0: m['expert'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else ''
        if 'PerГӯodo:' in r0: m['period'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else ''
        if 'DepГіsito inicial:' in r0: m['deposit'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
        if 'Beneficio Neto:' in r0:
            m['net_profit'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['abs_dd_balance'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else 0
        if 'Beneficio Bruto:' in r0:
            m['gross_profit'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['max_dd_balance_str'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else ''
            m['max_dd_equity_str'] = nn[5] if len(nn)>5 else ''
        if 'PГ©rdidas Brutas:' in r0:
            m['gross_loss'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['rel_dd_balance_str'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else ''
            m['rel_dd_equity_str'] = nn[5] if len(nn)>5 else ''
        if 'Factor de Beneficio:' in r0:
            m['pf'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['expected_payoff'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else 0
        if 'Factor de RecuperaciГіn:' in r0:
            m['recovery_factor'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['sharpe'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else 0
        if 'Total de operaciones ejecutadas:' in r0:
            m['total_trades'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['short_str'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else ''
            m['long_str'] = nn[5] if len(nn)>5 else ''
        if 'Posiciones rentables' in r0 and 'del total' in r0:
            m['win_str'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else ''
            m['loss_str'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else ''
        if 'La transacciГіn rentable:' in r0:
            m['max_win'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['max_loss'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else 0
        if 'Promedio de transacciГіn rentable:' in r0:
            m['avg_win'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else 0
            m['avg_loss'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else 0
        if 'El nГәmero mГЎximo de ganancias consecutivas' in r0:
            m['max_consec_wins_str'] = nn[1] if len(nn)>1 else ''
            m['max_consec_losses_str'] = nn[3] if len(nn)>3 else ''
        
        # Params
        for v in nn:
            vs = str(v)
            if '=' in vs and not vs.startswith('<') and len(vs) < 60:
                parts = vs.split('=', 1)
                if len(parts) == 2:
                    m[f'param_{parts[0].strip()}'] = parts[1].strip()
        
        # Trades
        if 'Г“rdenes' in r0:
            in_orders = True
        if in_orders and not header_found and 'Hora de apertura' in r0:
            header_found = True
            continue
        if header_found and len(nn) >= 9:
            try:
                date_str = str(nn[0])
                if re.match(r'\d{4}\.\d{2}\.\d{2}', date_str):
                    trade = {
                        'open_time': date_str,
                        'type': str(nn[3]),
                        'status': str(nn[9]) if len(nn)>9 else '',
                    }
                    trades.append(trade)
            except:
                pass
    
    # Compute win rate
    win_pct = extract_pct(m.get('win_str',''))
    m['win_rate'] = win_pct
    
    # Compute max DD
    m['max_dd_pct'] = extract_pct(m.get('max_dd_balance_str',''))
    m['max_dd_equity_pct'] = extract_pct(m.get('max_dd_equity_str',''))
    m['rel_dd_pct'] = extract_pct(m.get('rel_dd_balance_str',''))
    
    # Short vs long win rate
    m['short_wr'] = extract_pct(m.get('short_str',''))
    m['long_wr'] = extract_pct(m.get('long_str',''))
    
    # Return on deposit
    dep = m.get('deposit', 5000)
    if dep and dep != 0:
        m['return_pct'] = (m.get('net_profit',0) / dep) * 100
    
    # Risk/Reward
    avg_w = m.get('avg_win', 0)
    avg_l = abs(m.get('avg_loss', -1)) if m.get('avg_loss', 0) != 0 else 1
    m['rr_ratio'] = avg_w / avg_l if avg_l else 0
    
    # Trades summary
    m['trades'] = trades
    m['filled_trades'] = [t for t in trades if t['status'] == 'filled']
    m['filled_count'] = len(m['filled_trades'])
    
    return m

all_m = {}
for name, path in files.items():
    all_m[name] = extract_all_metrics(path)

# Summary table
print("\n{'='*100}")
print("FULL COMPARISON TABLE")
print("="*100)

header = f"{'Metric':<35} {'v1_S1_BK':>10} {'v1_S1_FR':>10} {'v1_S2_BK':>10} {'v1_S2_FR':>10} | {'v2_S1_BK':>10} {'v2_S1_FR':>10} {'v2_S2_BK':>10} {'v2_S2_FR':>10} {'v2_S3_BK':>10} {'v2_S3_FR':>10}"
print(header)
print("-"*150)

keys = [
    ('Net Profit ($)', 'net_profit', '${:.1f}'),
    ('Return (%)', 'return_pct', '{:.1f}%'),
    ('Gross Profit ($)', 'gross_profit', '${:.1f}'),
    ('Gross Loss ($)', 'gross_loss', '${:.1f}'),
    ('Profit Factor', 'pf', '{:.3f}'),
    ('Win Rate (%)', 'win_rate', '{:.1f}%'),
    ('Short WR (%)', 'short_wr', '{:.1f}%'),
    ('Long WR (%)', 'long_wr', '{:.1f}%'),
    ('Total Trades', 'total_trades', '{:.0f}'),
    ('Filled Trades', 'filled_count', '{:.0f}'),
    ('Max DD % (Bal)', 'max_dd_pct', '{:.2f}%'),
    ('Max DD % (Eq)', 'max_dd_equity_pct', '{:.2f}%'),
    ('Recovery Factor', 'recovery_factor', '{:.3f}'),
    ('Sharpe Ratio', 'sharpe', '{:.3f}'),
    ('Avg Win ($)', 'avg_win', '${:.2f}'),
    ('Avg Loss ($)', 'avg_loss', '${:.2f}'),
    ('RR Ratio', 'rr_ratio', '{:.2f}'),
    ('Exp Payoff ($)', 'expected_payoff', '${:.3f}'),
]

order = ['v1_SET1_BK','v1_SET1_FR','v1_SET2_BK','v1_SET2_FR','v2_SET1_BK','v2_SET1_FR','v2_SET2_BK','v2_SET2_FR','v2_SET3_BK','v2_SET3_FR']

for label, key, fmt in keys:
    row_str = f"{label:<35}"
    for n in order:
        val = all_m[n].get(key)
        if val is not None:
            try:
                row_str += f" {fmt.format(val):>10}"
            except:
                row_str += f" {str(val):>10}"
        else:
            row_str += f" {'N/A':>10}"
        if n == 'v1_SET2_FR':
            row_str += " |"
    print(row_str)

print("\n\nPARAMETERS")
print("="*100)
param_keys = ['InpRangeCandles','InpSessionHour','InpTP_Pips','InpSL_Pips','InpBE_Pips',
              'InpSPS_Enabled','InpSPS_Threshold','InpSPS_LookbackCandles','InpSPS_UseRecencyBias',
              'InpAllowThursday']
for pk in param_keys:
    row_str = f"{'param_'+pk:<35}"
    for n in order:
        val = all_m[n].get(f'param_{pk}', 'N/A')
        row_str += f" {str(val):>10}"
        if n == 'v1_SET2_FR':
            row_str += " |"
    print(row_str)

EOF

order = ['v1_SET1_BK','v1_SET1_FR','v1_SET2_BK','v1_SET2_FR','v2_SET1_BK','v2_SET1_FR','v2_SET2_BK','v2_SET2_FR','v2_SET3_BK','v2_SET3_FR']

for label, key, fmt in keys:
    row_str = f"{label:<35}"
    for n in order:
        val = all_m[n].get(key)
        if val is not None:
            try:
                row_str += f" {fmt.format(val):>10}"
            except:
                row_str += f" {str(val):>10}"
        else:
            row_str += f" {'N/A':>10}"
        if n == 'v1_SET2_FR':
            row_str += " |"
    print(row_str)

print("\n\nPARAMETERS")
print("="*100)
param_keys = ['InpRangeCandles','InpSessionHour','InpTP_Pips','InpSL_Pips','InpBE_Pips',
              'InpSPS_Enabled','InpSPS_Threshold','InpSPS_LookbackCandles','InpSPS_UseRecencyBias',
              'InpAllowThursday']
for pk in param_keys:
    row_str = f"{'param_'+pk:<35}"
    for n in order:
        val = all_m[n].get(f'param_{pk}', 'N/A')
        row_str += f" {str(val):>10}"
        if n == 'v1_SET2_FR':
            row_str += " |"
    print(row_str)

EOF
"}

content.thinking:
Now I have all the data I need to do a comprehensive analysis. Let me also get the monthly/yearly breakdown from the trades to understand consistency. Let me extract trade-level data and compute per-year/per-month statistics.

Also notice that:
- v1 files are in lowercase: set1_bk, set1_fr, set2_bk, set2_fr
- v2 files are in UPPERCASE: SET_1_BK, SET_1_FR, SET_2_BK, SET_2_FR, SET_3_BK, SET_3_FR


Now I'm looking at the performance metrics for each configuration across both versions. The v1 strategy shows Set 1 performing well with strong Sharpe ratios and profit factors, while Set 2 appears to be cut off mid-analysis. Looking at Set 2 with a slightly higher breakeven point, the backtest shows marginal improvements in Sharpe ratio and drawdown, while the forward test performance dips slightly. Set 3 reduces the stop loss and adjusts the SPS lookback period, which increases trade frequency but lowers the profit factor and Sharpe ratio across both backtests.

Now comparing backtest versus forward test results, I notice the forward tests are what actually matter for prop firm challenges since they reflect real market conditions. The v1 forward test results surprisingly show better profit factor retention than backtest, but v2 shows consistent degradation across all three setsвҖ”roughly 81-83% of the backtest profit factor carries through to forward testing, which suggests the strategy may be overfitted to historical data.

The SPS filter helps v2 during backtesting but doesn't translate to forward test performance, and the reduced trade count from filtering weakens statistical reliability. What's particularly telling is that v2's SET1 and SET2 have nearly identical parameters with only the breakeven differing, indicating potential over-optimization. For prop firm requirements, drawdown control is critical, and while v2 shows tighter drawdown in backtest, the forward test drawdown spikes significantly in SET2, which is a red flag.

Looking at win rates across versions, they're all relatively modestвҖ”ranging from 31-45%вҖ”but what matters for prop firms is consistent positive expectancy rather than high win rate. The real standout is v1 SET2 in forward testing with a profit factor of 1.343 and manageable drawdown, suggesting genuine robustness rather than curve-fitting.

v2 shows promise in backtesting but deteriorates more significantly in forward testing, which hints the SPS filter might be capturing some overfitting or the reduced trade frequency is introducing more variance. I need to pull the win rate data from the profitable positions rows and then build out the dashboard to visualize the year-by-year consistency.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import re
from datetime import datetime

files = {
    "v1_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_fr.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_fr.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_FR.xlsx",
}

def get_trades_and_metrics(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    trades = []
    in_orders = False
    header_found = False
    win_str = ''
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        nn = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not nn:
            continue
        r0 = str(nn[0])
        
        # Get win rate string
        if 'Posiciones rentables (% del total)' in r0:
            win_str = str(nn[1]) if len(nn)>1 else ''
        
        if 'Г“rdenes' in r0:
            in_orders = True
        if in_orders and not header_found and 'Hora de apertura' in r0:
            header_found = True
            continue
        
        # Parse trade rows
        if header_found and len(nn) >= 9:
            date_str = str(nn[0])
            if re.match(r'\d{4}\.\d{2}\.\d{2}', date_str):
                try:
                    # Format: date, order, symbol, type, volume, price, SL, TP, close_time, status, comment
                    status = str(nn[9]) if len(nn)>9 else ''
                    close_time_str = str(nn[8]) if len(nn)>8 else ''
                    
                    # For deals section - we'll find the profit in the deals
                    trades.append({
                        'open_time': date_str,
                        'type': str(nn[3]),
                        'status': status,
                        'close_time': close_time_str,
                        'year': int(date_str[:4]),
                    })
                except:
                    pass
    
    # Extract win rate
    m = re.search(r'(\d+)\s*\((\d+\.?\d*)%\)', win_str)
    win_rate = float(m.group(2)) if m else None
    win_count = int(m.group(1)) if m else None
    
    return trades, win_rate, win_count

# Get win rates
for name, path in files.items():
    trades, wr, wc = get_trades_and_metrics(path)
    print(f"{name}: WR={wr}% (wins={wc}), filled_trades={len([t for t in trades if t['status']=='filled'])}")

# Now extract deals (not orders) for P&L per trade
print("\n\nDEALS SECTION EXTRACTION")
def get_deals(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    deals = []
    in_deals = False
    header_found = False
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        nn = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not nn:
            continue
        r0 = str(nn[0])
        
        if r0 == 'Transacciones':
            in_deals = True
        if in_deals and not header_found and 'Hora' in r0:
            header_found = True
            continue
        
        if header_found and len(nn) >= 8:
            date_str = str(nn[0])
            if re.match(r'\d{4}\.\d{2}\.\d{2}', date_str):
                try:
                    # Deals format: date, deal#, order#, symbol, type, direction, volume, price, order, comm, swap, profit, balance, comment
                    deal_type = str(nn[4]) if len(nn)>4 else ''
                    profit = nn[11] if len(nn)>11 else None
                    if profit is not None and isinstance(profit, (int, float)):
                        deals.append({
                            'date': date_str,
                            'year': int(date_str[:4]),
                            'type': deal_type,
                            'profit': float(profit),
                        })
                except Exception as e:
                    pass
    
    return deals

# Get per-year breakdown
print("\nPER-YEAR PROFIT BREAKDOWN:")
for name, path in files.items():
    deals = get_deals(path)
    trade_deals = [d for d in deals if d['type'] == 'out']
    
    year_profits = {}
    for d in trade_deals:
        yr = d['year']
        year_profits[yr] = year_profits.get(yr, 0) + d['profit']
    
    print(f"\n{name}: {len(trade_deals)} deals")
    for yr in sorted(year_profits.keys()):
        print(f"  {yr}: ${year_profits[yr]:.2f}")

EOF

# Now extract deals (not orders) for P&L per trade
print("\n\nDEALS SECTION EXTRACTION")
def get_deals(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    deals = []
    in_deals = False
    header_found = False
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        nn = [v for v in vals if pd.notna(v)]
        if not nn:
            continue
        r0 = str(nn[0])
        
        if r0 == 'Transacciones':
            in_deals = True
        if in_deals and not header_found and 'Hora' in r0:
            header_found = True
            continue
        
        if header_found and len(nn) >= 8:
            date_str = str(nn[0])
            if re.match(r'\d{4}\.\d{2}\.\d{2}', date_str):
                try:
                    # Deals format: date, deal#, order#, symbol, type, direction, volume, price, order, comm, swap, profit, balance, comment
                    deal_type = str(nn[4]) if len(nn)>4 else ''
                    profit = nn[11] if len(nn)>11 else None
                    if profit is not None and isinstance(profit, (int, float)):
                        deals.append({
                            'date': date_str,
                            'year': int(date_str[:4]),
                            'type': deal_type,
                            'profit': float(profit),
                        })
                except Exception as e:
                    pass
    
    return deals

# Get per-year breakdown
print("\nPER-YEAR PROFIT BREAKDOWN:")
for name, path in files.items():
    deals = get_deals(path)
    trade_deals = [d for d in deals if d['type'] == 'out']
    
    year_profits = {}
    for d in trade_deals:
        yr = d['year']
        year_profits[yr] = year_profits.get(yr, 0) + d['profit']
    
    print(f"\n{name}: {len(trade_deals)} deals")
    for yr in sorted(year_profits.keys()):
        print(f"  {yr}: ${year_profits[yr]:.2f}")

EOF
"}

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import re

files = {
    "v1_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set1_fr.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_bk.xlsx",
    "v1_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/set2_fr.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET1_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_1_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET2_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_2_FR.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_BK": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_BK.xlsx",
    "v2_SET3_FR": "/mnt/user-data/uploads/SET_3_FR.xlsx",
}

def get_trades(path):
    df = pd.read_excel(path, header=None)
    trades = []
    in_deals = False
    header_found = False
    
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        vals = list(row)
        if pd.isna(vals[0]):
            continue
        r0 = str(vals[0])
        
        if r0 == 'Transacciones':
            in_deals = True
            continue
        if in_deals and not header_found and str(vals[0]) == 'Fecha/Hora':
            header_found = True
            continue
        
        if header_found:
            date_str = str(vals[0])
            if re.match(r'\d{4}\.\d{2}\.\d{2}', date_str):
                direction = vals[4] if pd.notna(vals[4]) else ''
                profit = vals[10] if pd.notna(vals[10]) else 0
                
                if str(direction) == 'out':
                    trades.append({
                        'date': date_str,
                        'year': int(date_str[:4]),
                        'month': int(date_str[5:7]),
                        'profit': float(profit) if isinstance(profit, (int, float)) else 0,
                        'win': float(profit) > 0 if isinstance(profit, (int, float)) else False,
                    })
    
    return trades

# Compute per-year stats
print("PER-YEAR BREAKDOWN (USD profit per year)")
print("="*100)

all_trades = {}
for name, path in files.items():
    trades = get_trades(path)
    all_trades[name] = trades
    
    wins = [t for t in trades if t['win']]
    losses = [t for t in trades if not t['win']]
    wr = len(wins)/len(trades)*100 if trades else 0
    
    year_profits = {}
    year_counts = {}
    for t in trades:
        yr = t['year']
        year_profits[yr] = year_profits.get(yr, 0) + t['profit']
        year_counts[yr] = year_counts.get(yr, 0) + 1
    
    print(f"\n{name}: Total={len(trades)}, WR={wr:.1f}%")
    for yr in sorted(year_profits.keys()):
        sign = "вң…" if year_profits[yr] > 0 else "вқҢ"
        print(f"  {yr}: ${year_profits[yr]:+.2f} ({year_counts[yr]} trades) {sign}")

# Also check win rates
print("\n\nWIN RATES FROM DEALS:")
for name, trades in all_trades.items():
    wins = [t for t in trades if t['win']]
    be = [t for t in trades if t['profit'] == 0]
    losses = [t for t in trades if not t['win'] and t['profit'] != 0]
    wr = len(wins)/len(trades)*100 if trades else 0
    print(f"{name}: Trades={len(trades)}, Wins={len(wins)}, BE={len(be)}, Losses={len(losses)}, WR={wr:.1f}%")

EOF

print("="*100)

all_trades = {}
for name, path in files.items():
    trades = get_trades(path)
    all_trades[name] = trades
    
    wins = [t for t in trades if t['win']]
    losses = [t for t in trades if not t['win']]
    wr = len(wins)/len(trades)*100 if trades else 0
    
    year_profits = {}
    year_counts = {}
    for t in trades:
        yr = t['year']
        year_profits[yr] = year_profits.get(yr, 0) + t['profit']
        year_counts[yr] = year_counts.get(yr, 0) + 1
    
    print(f"\n{name}: Total={len(trades)}, WR={wr:.1f}%")
    for yr in sorted(year_profits.keys()):
        sign = "✅" if year_profits[yr] > 0 else "❌"
        print(f"  {yr}: ${year_profits[yr]:+.2f} ({year_counts[yr]} trades) {sign}")

# Also check win rates
print("\n\nWIN RATES FROM DEALS:")
for name, trades in all_trades.items():
    wins = [t for t in trades if t['win']]
    be = [t for t in trades if t['profit'] == 0]
    losses = [t for t in trades if not t['win'] and t['profit'] != 0]
    wr = len(wins)/len(trades)*100 if trades else 0
    print(f"{name}: Trades={len(trades)}, Wins={len(wins)}, BE={len(be)}, Losses={len(losses)}, WR={wr:.1f}%")

EOF
"}

content.thinking:
Excellent! Now I have very complete data. Let me analyze this carefully:

**Key Findings:**

**Win Rates (actual from deals):**
- v1 SET1: BK=37.7%, FR=39.0% вҶҗ Very consistent, FR вүҲ BK вң…
- v1 SET2: BK=35.1%, FR=38.9% вҶҗ FR > BK вң… (robustness signal)
- v2 SET1: BK=27.2%, FR=23.3% вҶҗ FR < BK вҡ пёҸ degradation


- v2 SET2: BK=28.0%, FR=24.3% вҶҗ FR < BK вҡ пёҸ degradation
- v2 SET3: BK=22.5%, FR=18.2% вҶҗ FR < BK рҹҡЁ significant degradation

There's a discrepancy between the MT5 strategy tester results (showing 44-45% for v1) and my computed win rates from the actual deals. The MT5 report might be counting profitable positions differentlyвҖ”possibly treating break-even trades or canceled orders differently than how I'm calculating from the deal data.

Looking at the MT5 report numbers more closely, I see the issue: MT5 counts each order separately (both entry and exit), so 236 trades generate 472 transactions. The 106 profitable positions MT5 reports versus my 89 winsвҖ”that 17-trade difference matches exactly with my break-even count, meaning MT5 is including break-even trades as profitable while I'm counting them separately.

For prop firm evaluation, break-even trades don't really matter since they don't impact P&L, so what matters is hitting the daily drawdown limit (usually 4-5%), total drawdown (8-10%), reaching the net profit target (8-10%), and maintaining consistency without blowing up any single period.

Looking at the year-by-year breakdown across all versions, v1 SET1 and SET2 both show consistent profitability in backtesting and forward testing periods, with SET2 forward testing particularly strong at +$2856 in 2025. v2 SET1 is just starting to show results. Looking at the rest of v2 SET3 FR results and the forward testing data, I'm noticing a clear pattern in how these strategies perform out-of-sample. The backtesting results look strong across the board, but when I compare them to forward testing, there's some degradation in the profit factorsвҖ”v2 SET1 shows a concerning 18% drop, while v1 SET2 actually improved, which is interesting.

The win rates tell an even starker story. V1 holds steady or improves slightly in forward testing, but v2 shows consistent deterioration across all three sets, with SET3 dropping from 22.5% down to 18.2%вҖ”that's a significant decline that suggests potential overfitting in the v2 models.

Now I'm checking these results against prop firm requirements. Most firms like FundingPips enforce strict drawdown limitsвҖ”typically 4-5% daily and 8-10% totalвҖ”with profit targets in that same 8-10% range. Looking at the maximum drawdown metrics, v1 SET1 is already pushing it at 11.82% on balance, which exceeds the 8% threshold, and the equity drawdown of 12.77% is even worse. V1 SET2 follows a similar pattern with 11.66% balance drawdown. Even the forward testing results for v1 are marginal at best, sitting right at or slightly above the acceptable limits.

V2 shows more promise in certain configurations. V2 SET1 on the backtest hits 8.27% balance drawdown with 8.57% equityвҖ”just barely within tolerance. V2 SET2 backtest is cleaner at 8.13% balance and 8.43% equity. But the forward results are inconsistent; V2 SET1 FR spikes to 10.76% equity drawdown, and V2 SET3 FR is completely unacceptable at 14.16% balance and 12.68% equity. The pattern emerging is that v1 SET2 appears to be the most stable across both backtest and forward periods, maintaining more consistent performance without the wild swings I'm seeing in the v2 variants.

The core issue is that v2's lower win ratesвҖ”sitting between 18-28%вҖ”create psychological strain and expose the strategy to brutal losing streaks that prop firms won't tolerate. V1's 35-39% win rate is far more sustainable even if the reward-to-risk ratios aren't as sharp. V2 compensates with better risk-reward (2.42-2.65 versus v1's 1.51-2.17), but that advantage evaporates when the strategy can't maintain consistency in live trading.

The break-even mechanism in v2 is also working against itвҖ”SET3 FR shows nearly 20% of trades hitting break-even, which suggests the BE_pips threshold is too tight and stops out positions before they can reach target. For a prop firm challenge, v1 SET2 FR looks like the safer bet if I strip out the Thursday filter and dial back position sizing to keep drawdown under control.

Now I'm looking at the file naming convention more carefully. The BK and FR designations seem to split the data chronologicallyвҖ”BK files cluster around 2024-2025 while FR files show 2025-2026 activity, which would make BK the in-sample period and FR the forward/out-of-sample period, even though the strategy tester reports the same overall date range for both. I think I've figured it outвҖ”BK and FR must be running on different date ranges despite the headers showing the same period. The BK files concentrated trades in 2024 with just a handful in 2025, while FR loaded up on 2025-2026 trades, which suggests BK was the in-sample period (roughly Jan 2024 through early 2025) and FR was the forward/out-of-sample period (Jan 2025 through Feb 2026). header shows the full testing range, but the actual trades are concentrated in different periods.

Actually, re-reading the doc about v2: "IS: 2024вҖ“2021, OOS: 2022вҖ“2023, Walk-Forward: 2024вҖ“present" - but this was for the Donchian Channel EA. For the ORB EA:
- BK = IS (backtesting)
- FR = Forward testing (demo account testing)

So:
- BK period: 2024 (roughly)
- FR period: 2025-2026 (forward testing, more recent)

This makes sense! The BK files test on 2024 data (IS), and the FR files test on 2025-2026 data (OOS/forward).

Now everything makes sense:

**IS (BK) - Training period: 2024**
**OOS (FR) - Live/Forward period: 2025-2026**

**Key Metric: ISвҶ’OOS stability**

Looking at the performance comparison, v1 SET2 shows the strongest forward results with $2844 net profit and a 1.343 profit factor despite a 9.10% drawdown, while v2 SET1 is more conservative with $1622 net but better drawdown control at 8.85%. The critical insight is that v1 SET2 actually performs better in forward testing than backtesting, suggesting genuine robustness, whereas v2 looks cleaner in-sample but deteriorates out-of-sample. For a prop firm challenge with typical requirements like 8% profit target, 5% daily drawdown limit, and 8-10% total drawdown cap, I need to evaluate which configuration has the better risk-adjusted odds of passing. Looking at the equity drawdown metrics instead of balance drawdowns, all versions exceed the 8% threshold that most prop firms requireвҖ”ranging from 10.15% up to 12.68%вҖ”which means none of these setups would actually qualify for standard prop firm challenges as they currently stand.

The path forward is clear: reducing position sizes by 30-40% would bring the drawdowns into acceptable range, and v1 SET2 remains the strongest candidate given its superior out-of-sample performance despite the higher equity DD.

Now I'm building out the dashboard visualization.

content.text: # Imagine вҖ” Visual Creation Suite ## Modules Call read_me again with the modules parameter to load detailed guidance: - `diagram` вҖ” SVG flowcharts, structural diagrams, illustrative diagrams - `mockup` вҖ” UI mockups, forms, cards, dashboards - `interactive` вҖ” interactive explainers with controls - `chart` вҖ” charts, data analysis, geographic maps (Chart.js, D3 choropleth) - `art` вҖ” illustration and generative art Pick the closest fit. The module includes all relevant design guidance. **Complexity budget вҖ” hard limits:** - Box subtitles: вүӨ5 words. Detail goes in click-through (`sendPrompt`) or the prose below вҖ” not the box. - Colors: вүӨ2 ramps per diagram. If colors encode meaning (states, tiers), add a 1-line legend. Otherwise use one neutral ramp. - Horizontal tier: вүӨ4 boxes at full width (~140px each). 5+ boxes вҶ’ shrink to вүӨ110px OR wrap to 2 rows OR split into overview + detail diagrams. If you catch yourself writing "click to learn more" in prose, the diagram itself must ACTUALLY be sparse. Don't promise brevity then front-load everything. You create rich visual content вҖ” SVG diagrams/illustrations and HTML interactive widgets вҖ” that renders inline in conversation. The best output feels like a natural extension of the chat. ## Core Design System These rules apply to ALL use cases. ### Philosophy - **Seamless**: Users shouldn't notice where claude.ai ends and your widget begins. - **Flat**: No gradients, mesh backgrounds, noise textures, or decorative effects. Clean flat surfaces. - **Compact**: Show the essential inline. Explain the rest in text. - **Text goes in your response, visuals go in the tool** вҖ” All explanatory text, descriptions, introductions, and summaries must be written as normal response text OUTSIDE the tool call. The tool output should contain ONLY the visual element (diagram, chart, interactive widget). Never put paragraphs of explanation, section headings, or descriptive prose inside the HTML/SVG. If the user asks "explain X", write the explanation in your response and use the tool only for the visual that accompanies it. The user's font settings only apply to your response text, not to text inside the widget. ### Streaming Output streams token-by-token. Structure code so useful content appears early. - **HTML**: `
